B.Tech Data Science · Statistical Study · 2024

Counting Steps,
Measuring
Energy

A longitudinal study of 378 records bridging sensor physics and biological variance — examining the statistical relationship between gait-based locomotion and metabolic expenditure.

Total Records 378
Participants 14
Pearson r 0.014
∑
01 — Data Origin & Personnel

The Cohort

Fourteen participants contributed longitudinal activity and calorie data captured via the Step Counter Pedometer App, each tracked across multiple sessions to build a robust 378-record dataset.

📱

Step Counter Pedometer App

Primary data collection instrument. Records daily step counts, distance, activity duration, and caloric expenditure estimates with timestamp precision across all 14 participants.

⚙️

MEMS Tri-Axial Accelerometer

Micro-Electro-Mechanical System hardware engine. Captures raw acceleration vectors along three orthogonal axes (X, Y, Z) at sampling frequencies sufficient to resolve individual gait cycles.

🐍

Python Statistical Pipeline

Analysis performed using Pandas, NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib. Dual-track manual audit cross-validated computational results to within 0.01% relative error.

⬡ View Google Sites Data Repository →
02 — Sensor Physics

How Motion Becomes Data

The translation of raw inertial signals into meaningful step counts relies on a fundamental computation from vector algebra applied to MEMS accelerometer output.

01

Tri-Axial Sampling

The MEMS accelerometer continuously samples gravitational acceleration components along three mutually perpendicular axes — capturing the full three-dimensional signature of each stride.

02

Resultant Vector Magnitude

Raw axis readings are collapsed into a single scalar magnitude using the Euclidean norm. This removes directional ambiguity, making step detection orientation-agnostic regardless of device placement.

03

Peak Detection & Thresholding

A dynamic threshold algorithm identifies periodical peaks in the magnitude signal, each corresponding to the heel-strike phase of a complete gait cycle — one peak equals one step.

04

Calorie Estimation

Step count is combined with participant anthropometry — body mass, stride length — to estimate metabolic expenditure in kilocalories using established MET-equivalent models.

Resultant Vector Magnitude
X — Lateral Y — Longitudinal Z — Vertical
03 — Global Statistical Moments

The Cohort Profile

Aggregate distributional properties of the full 378-record dataset reveal a near-symmetrical, broad-distribution activity profile — a platykurtic population that defies single-modal assumptions.

👣
7,587
Mean Daily Steps
Cohort-wide average across all 378 activity records. Slightly above global recommended thresholds of ~7,000 steps/day.
.3 steps/day (μ)
⚖️
0.081
Skewness
A value near zero confirms the distribution of activity counts is remarkably symmetrical around its central tendency — no significant tail dominance.
Near-Symmetrical
📊
−1.077
Kurtosis
Negative excess kurtosis indicates a platykurtic distribution — flatter than normal, with activity broadly spread rather than concentrated near the mean.
Platykurtic / Broad Activity
04 — The Metabolic Paradox
⚠ Key Finding

The Discovery

Against intuitive expectation, the regression analysis of steps versus caloric output produced a striking null result — a finding with profound implications for biometric modeling.

Pearson Correlation
r
Steps ↔ Calories
Coefficient of Determination
R²
Variance Explained
Model Type
SLR
Simple Linear Regression

The regression analysis indicates no significant linear relationship, proving human metabolism is too complex for single-variable modeling.

Interpretation: An r value of 0.014 is indistinguishable from zero correlation. The R² of 0.000 means step count accounts for effectively none of the variance in caloric output across the cohort. This is not instrument error — it is a genuine reflection of biological complexity: factors including metabolic rate, body composition, terrain, incline, cadence variability, and individual fitness adaptations all contribute independently to total energy expenditure.

05 — Manual Audit vs. Digital

The Authenticity Check

A dual-track audit on Participant Dharani's data validates the Python pipeline's precision against hand-calculated statistical measures — confirming computational integrity.

Dual Track Audit
Participant: Dharani
Manual Correlation (r) −0.44
Global Cohort r +0.014
Manual vs. Python Error < 0.01%
Calculation Method Pearson Product-Moment
Individual vs Cohort Diverges Significantly
✓ Python matched manual within 0.01% relative error

Why Dharani Diverges

Dharani's individual r of −0.44 — a negative correlation between steps and calories — appears paradoxical until individual context is considered. Her sessions show higher-calorie outputs at moderate step counts, likely attributable to session intensity, activity type (strength vs. walking), or metabolic idiosyncrasies.

This phenomenon, ecological fallacy in reverse, illustrates why cohort-level aggregation obscures inter-individual variation — the central motivation for moving toward person-specific MLR models.

AUDIT VERDICT
Pipeline Validated ✓
All hand calculations matched Python output within accepted precision bounds.
06 — Future Intelligence

Towards Multiple Regression

The null SLR result motivates a higher-dimensional model incorporating biometric predictors. Multiple Linear Regression transforms the problem from a line to a plane.

X₁

Daily Step Count

Gait-based locomotion output — the primary predictor retained from SLR phase.

X₂

Heart Rate (BPM)

Cardiovascular load intensity — the single strongest physiological proxy for metabolic rate during activity.

X₃

Body Mass Index (BMI)

Anthropometric baseline controlling for mass-dependent caloric cost — crucial for cross-participant comparability.

ε

Residual Error Term

Captures unexplained biological variance — acknowledging that no finite-predictor model fully encodes human metabolism.

INTERCEPT
β₀ — Baseline caloric output independent of predictors
HYPOTHESIS

Introducing HR and BMI as co-predictors is expected to elevate R² from near-zero to a statistically meaningful value (estimated > 0.45), validating the multi-dimensional nature of bioenergetic systems.

⚑ Ethics Statement

All data collected under Informed Consent protocols. Participants were briefed on the study scope, data usage, and their right to withdraw. No personally identifiable biometric data is published without explicit written permission. Data is anonymized in aggregate analysis and stored on secured institutional repositories only.
